data.title: If You Believe In Transformative AI Healthcare Stocks Then This Trio Resets Your Horizon Trajectory
data.description:
Artificial intelligence in healthcare is one of the clearest ways technology meets real world need, especially as higher energy prices, geopolitical tension, and sticky inflation keep pressure on costs and productivity. While many sectors face softer activity and rising input prices, AI tools that support better diagnostics, smoother hospital operations, and faster drug discovery speak directly to efficiency and outcomes. This screener focuses on companies applying AI to these problems, helping you focus on businesses tied to long term shifts in how care is delivered. Below, you will see 3 stocks from the Transformative AI Healthcare Stocks list.

Advertisement

Medtronic (MDT)

Overview: Medtronic is a large medical device company that supplies surgeons and clinicians with products such as heart pacemakers and defibrillators, spinal and brain implants, surgical tools and robotics, and insulin pumps and glucose monitors for diabetes care across the United States and international markets.

Operations: Medtronic generates most of its revenue from Cardiovascular (US$13.5b), Neuroscience (US$10.2b), Medical Surgical (US$8.6b), and Diabetes (US$3.0b), with the United States contributing US$17.8b and the rest of the world US$17.6b.

Market Cap: US$110.8b

Investors looking at AI in healthcare may find Medtronic interesting because its core cardiac, neurosurgical, and surgical device franchises are increasingly tied to software, data, and robotics, from AI powered surgical video platforms to the Stealth AXiS navigation system and ALERT trial work in heart valve care. The company backs this with sizeable R&D, a long dividend record at a 3.29% yield, and earnings that analysts expect to grow faster than its recent 5 year trend. At the same time, competition in diabetes tech, product recall history, and reliance on external borrowing keep risk on the table. The full picture of how these strengths and pressures fit together is where the real opportunity could lie for you.

Medtronic’s AI linked devices, robotics, and software could tell a different story than its headline dividend yield suggests. See how analyst expectations line up in the analyst forecasts for Medtronic and what might be hiding beneath them.

Insmed (INSM)

Overview: Insmed focuses on therapies for serious and rare diseases, with an approved lung infection treatment, ARIKAYCE, and a deep pipeline targeting bronchiectasis, pulmonary hypertension, Duchenne muscular dystrophy, amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, and other hard to treat conditions.

Operations: Insmed generates about US$606.4m in revenue from therapies for rare disease patients, with roughly US$453.0m from the United States and US$153.5m from international markets.

Market Cap: US$35.2b

Insmed stands out in AI focused healthcare because its core respiratory and rare disease portfolio is already commercial, yet still closely tied to upcoming data and approvals that could reshape the business, from brensocatib in bronchiectasis to TPIP in pulmonary hypertension and potential ARIKAYCE label expansion. Analysts highlight revenue and earnings growth potential, but this depends on approvals, payer support, and successful market access, while the company remains loss making and reliant on external funding and fresh equity. For investors willing to take on that risk, the combination of multiple late stage programs, expanding international reach, and analyst targets presents a story that is more layered than a simple high growth biotech label suggests.

Insmed’s late stage pipeline and rare disease focus may be masking a broader story about how all those moving parts fit together. See how the analysis report for Insmed explains the potential upside along with the risks that could change the outlook.

Stryker (SYK)

Overview: Stryker is a global medical technology company that supplies hospitals and clinicians with surgical equipment, neurotechnology devices, AI assisted virtual care and communication platforms, and orthopaedic implants for joint replacement and trauma care.

Operations: Stryker generates about US$9.5b from Orthopaedics and US$15.6b from MedSurg and Neurotechnology, with most revenue coming from the United States and additional contributions from Europe, the Middle East, Africa, Asia Pacific, and other international markets.

Market Cap: US$127.2b

Stryker sits at the intersection of hardware and software in healthcare, combining orthopaedic implants and smart robotics like Mako with AI assisted hospital and virtual care platforms. It carries debt on its balance sheet, and recent cyberattacks highlight how exposed connected hospital suppliers can be. Earnings, return on equity forecasts and ongoing investment in connected operating rooms, stroke care and digital SmartHospital services indicate a business that is still evolving. For investors interested in AI health tools backed by an established, profitable medical device franchise, the key consideration is how that mix of growth, risk and valuation fits together beyond the headlines.

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value.

Approach 1: Gen Digital Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes the cash that a company is expected to generate in the future and discounts those projections back to today to estimate what the business might be worth right now.

For Gen Digital, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections expressed in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.52b. Analysts provide detailed estimates for the next few years, and Simply Wall St then extrapolates beyond that, with projected free cash flow of $1.98b in 2035 according to the ten year schedule supplied.

Pulling all of those discounted cash flows together produces an estimated intrinsic value of $34.72 per share. Compared with the current share price, the DCF output suggests the stock is around 44.8% below that estimate. This indicates that Gen Digital is trading at a meaningful discount to this cash flow based valuation.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Gen Digital is undervalued by 44.8%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 61 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Gen Digital.

Approach 2: Gen Digital Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to see how much you are paying for each dollar of earnings. It reflects what the market is currently willing to pay for the business today based on its earnings power.

Higher growth expectations and lower perceived risk often line up with a higher P/E ratio, while slower expected growth or higher risk tend to be associated with a lower P/E. So what counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E really depends on what investors expect from a company’s earnings and how confident they feel about those earnings.

Gen Digital currently trades on a P/E of 19.26x. This sits below the Software industry average P/E of 30.09x and also below the peer average of 24.87x. Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Gen Digital is 27.10x, which is the P/E level that its model suggests is reasonable given factors like earnings growth, profit margins, industry, market cap and company specific risks.

The Fair Ratio aims to be more tailored than a simple comparison with peers or the broad industry, because it adjusts for the company’s own growth profile, risk characteristics and profitability rather than assuming one size fits all. Comparing the current 19.26x P/E with the 27.10x Fair Ratio points to the shares trading below that model based reference point.
